summary: A proposed House bill (HR1737) would strip the CFPB of its ability to enforce anti-discrimination rules against auto lenders who charge minorities higher dealer markups. The CFPB has been holding banks accountable for discriminatory lending practices using disparate impact theory, but Republicans argue the methodology for detecting racism is flawed. O'Neil argues this is a dangerous position—that imperfect measurement shouldn't excuse ignoring discrimination entirely. She notes that the real goal should be eliminating racist practices, not just compensating victims, and points to BB&T's switch to flat fees as a model solution. The post frames this as an early test case for algorithmic auditing of institutional discrimination.
key_points: Dealer markups—discretionary fees added after credit risk assessment—are consistently higher for Black and Hispanic borrowers, constituting illegal discrimination under disparate impact theory
Ally Financial doesn't deny their loans were racist; they only dispute the degree of racism and the measurement methodology
The Republican argument that imperfect racism measurement means no enforcement is exactly what disparate impact law was designed to prevent
The statistical objection about false positives (whites miscategorized as minorities) would actually dilute measured discrimination, not inflate it
BB&T's switch to flat fees shows the real solution: eliminate discretionary practices that enable discrimination
money_quotes: Ally Financial doesn't claim their loans weren't racist. They simply claim that they were less racist than the CFPB thinks.
The Republican argument is: if you can't specify exactly how racist this practice is, then you can't stop people from doing it at all.

In statistical terms, we are worried about false positives – white people who might receive a compensation check for racist practices – and the proposed solution is to ignore all the actual victims of racist practices – so all the true positives. It's throwing the baby out with the bathwater.
The actual goal is to put an end to the racist policies themselves.
Pushback against those policies is a vote to keep shady, discriminatory negotiations with car dealers that, generally speaking, screw minorities.

This is just the beginning of a much larger debate around what kind of processes we can audit with algorithms, what constitutes evidence of discrimination, and how quickly – or slowly – the laws are going to catch up with technology.
It's a litmus test, and it's on the verge of failing badly.
key_insight: Demanding perfect measurement of discrimination before allowing any enforcement is a strategy for permitting discrimination to continue unchecked.
